speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome to Git Going with GitHub. I'm Alex.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I'm Jamie. If you're arriving with a workshop link, a little uncertainty, or that feeling of, wait, what am I supposed to do first, this is for you.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. This opening lesson is your orientation. We're going to make the path clear from the start: what this workshop is, how the two days fit together, how your private Learning Room works, how to get into it through GitHub Classroom, what to do when tools change, how to choose the right tool for the moment, what to listen for with a screen reader, and where to go for help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is not just a welcome message. It's the map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. And one of the most important promises in this course is that you are not being asked to do pretend work. This workshop is built around a real contribution path in real tools: GitHub, VS Code, Git, GitHub Copilot, and accessibility-focused contribution workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the big picture. This course guide describes the GitHub Learning Room as your workshop companion. Think of it as your base camp and table of contents for the whole experience.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's a note learners should hear early: the materials are actively refined.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Content may be updated leading up to and even during the course. That's not a warning that things are broken. It's a reminder that this is a living curriculum built around living platforms.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which leads to the other big reminder: tools and websites change.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do. GitHub.com changes. GitHub Classroom changes. VS Code changes. Copilot changes. Browser extensions change. Agent features change. Labels move, buttons get renamed, preview features appear or disappear, account policies differ, and model availability can shift.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That can feel stressful, especially if you're using a screen reader and following instructions carefully.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. So the course teaches a durable strategy, not just a frozen screenshot. If the docs and your screen differ, orient yourself by the URL, the browser tab title, the page heading, landmarks, tab names, button labels, keyboard help, and in VS Code, the Command Palette.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So instead of thinking, the page changed, I'm lost, you think, what signals tell me where I am now?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That skill matters more than memorizing one version of a page. And if you find a mismatch, report it so the curriculum can be corrected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Okay, so what is the workshop structure?

speaker: ALEX
text: It's a two-day workshop with one central idea: you will make real contributions to a real open source project. Not simulated. Not practice in a fake interface. Real collaboration skills in real environments.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But the live event and the written curriculum are not exactly the same size, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. The live agenda is intentionally smaller than the full self-paced curriculum. In the live sessions, facilitators prioritize the minimum core path needed for you to make and understand a real contribution. Then the rest remains available for preparation, catch-up, remote participation, and continued work after the event.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So there's a live core, and then there's asynchronous follow-up.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If something isn't covered live, that doesn't mean it's optional in the sense of unimportant. It means the full chapter set, challenge flow, solutions, podcast episodes, and Slack support are there so you can keep going. Remote participants also use the same checkpoints and evidence prompts, with written instructions available before each live block.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Walk us through the two days.

speaker: ALEX
text: Day 1 stays in the browser. You learn GitHub's web interface using only your keyboard and screen reader. The live Day 1 core path gets you through repository navigation, issues, branches, commits, and a first pull request.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then there's more beyond the minimum live path.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Review practice, merge conflicts, labels, notifications, and open source culture can be stretch goals during the live event or completed later asynchronously.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then Day 2 moves to the desktop.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Day 2 is Visual Studio Code plus accessibility agents. You move from the browser into VS Code, learn GitHub Copilot, and work with the Accessibility Agents ecosystem. The live core prepares you to make a real contribution, and the async continuation gives you time to improve and submit that contribution well.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that there's a progression here, not a leap.

speaker: ALEX
text: That progression is very intentional. First you learn the skill manually in the browser. Navigate a repository. Work with an issue. Open a pull request. Review. Merge.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then there's a bridge.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. On many GitHub repositories, you can press the period key to open github.dev, which is VS Code in your browser. It uses familiar keyboard patterns and a VS Code-style editor without installation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: But it has limits.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. No local terminal, no debugger in the full desktop sense, only web-compatible extensions, and not the complete desktop agent workflow.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So github.dev is a bridge, not the finish line.

speaker: ALEX
text: Well said. Once you understand the workflow in the browser, the desktop version makes more sense. Then on Day 2 you deepen the work: VS Code basics, Copilot inline suggestions, Copilot Chat, and agent-supported workflows like daily briefings, issue tracking, pull request review, analytics, and preparing an upstream contribution.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there's a principle underneath all that.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Learn the manual skill first. Then learn what automation is doing for you. Agents are helpful only if you understand the workflow they're accelerating.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before learners even begin Day 1, what should already be done?

speaker: ALEX
text: Two things matter here. First, if you want the most guided path, start with the Get Going with GitHub guide. That's the step-by-step onboarding path from getting the assignment link to knowing where to click, what challenge to open, and how to ask for help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And second?

speaker: ALEX
text: Complete the pre-workshop setup chapter before Day 1. That includes creating a GitHub account, installing Git, setting up VS Code -- optional for Day 1, required for Day 2 -- configuring your screen reader for GitHub, and verifying that everything works.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How long should that setup take?

speaker: ALEX
text: About thirty minutes, though support is available if it takes longer. And if you can't finish every setup step before the workshop, tell a facilitator early. Setup problems are normal.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also an audio layer to the course itself, which is fitting, since we are currently in it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Every chapter and appendix has a companion podcast episode. You can listen before reading to preview the concepts, or after reading to reinforce them. There's a browseable podcast page with HTML5 players, and there's an RSS feed if you prefer a podcast app.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the episodes are short enough to fit into real life.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, usually about eight to eighteen minutes. Good for a walk, a commute, or just a break from intensive screen reader navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the curriculum concrete. What does Day 1 cover?

speaker: ALEX
text: Day 1 begins with the getting-started path and pre-workshop setup, then moves into choosing your tools, understanding GitHub's web structure, navigating repositories, understanding the Learning Room, working with issues, working with pull requests, handling merge conflicts, open source culture, labels and milestones and projects, and finally notifications and a Day 1 close.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So by the end of Day 1, a learner should be able to use GitHub's web interface independently.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. The Day 1 self-paced total is about eight hours, but the live agenda focuses on the core path and may leave some chapters as async follow-up.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Day 2?

speaker: ALEX
text: Day 2 covers the VS Code interface and setup, accessibility features in VS Code, the mental model of Git, Git in practice, code review with pull requests and diffs, GitHub Copilot, issue templates, fork-based contribution, accessibility agents, a capstone project, and next steps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is a lot.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. About ten hours self-paced. Live Day 2 focuses on VS Code, Git, Copilot, agent discovery, and supported contribution work, with the deeper capstone often continuing afterward.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I also don't want to skip the reference materials, because those are a real accessibility support feature, not an afterthought.

speaker: ALEX
text: I agree. The appendices are there to stay open while you work. They are not part of the linear chapter sequence. They're meant to be used at the moment you need them.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the big ones learners should keep handy?

speaker: ALEX
text: Always-open resources include the glossary and the screen reader cheat sheet. Beyond that, the guide also points you to the FAQ, troubleshooting guide, quick reference, glossary, screen reader cheat sheet, and a broader resources collection.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if you hit jargon, use the glossary. If you forget a command, use quick reference. If something breaks, use troubleshooting.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And if you are still stuck, open a support issue at the Community Access support repository. Describe what you tried, what happened, what you expected, and include your screen reader and operating system.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That detail matters because support is much easier when the context is clear.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. "It doesn't work" is hard to diagnose. "I'm using NVDA on Windows, I activated this control, I expected a repository page, and instead I heard this" is actionable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we leave the course guide, can we do a compact workshop-at-a-glance recap?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. Day 1 focuses on the GitHub web interface using a browser and screen reader. The core skills are navigate, issue, pull request, review, and merge. The outcome is that you can use GitHub independently.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And Day 2 focuses on VS Code plus accessibility agents.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The tools include VS Code, Copilot, and the accessibility agent ecosystem. The core skills are Git, Copilot, agents, fork-based contribution, and the capstone path. The outcome is that you have a real contribution workflow and review process you can keep using.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone wants the official source of truth?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog. The course also includes section-level source maps so learners can verify facts by topic.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. Let's shift into the guided onboarding path: getting going with GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: This guide starts with a reassuring premise: if you are new, unsure, or joining from a workshop link, you do not need prior knowledge of Git, GitHub, VS Code, or the command line to begin.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's important to say out loud, because many learners assume everyone else already knows the basics.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The course is designed so you are not left guessing what comes next.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What specifically will learners have?

speaker: ALEX
text: You'll have a GitHub Classroom assignment link from the facilitator, your own private Learning Room repository, challenge issues with explicit instructions, evidence prompts telling you what to post when you finish, Gandalf bot feedback on pull requests, reference solutions you can compare against after you try, and multiple tool paths so you can work in the browser, VS Code, GitHub Desktop, or the command line when appropriate.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the goal isn't speed.

speaker: ALEX
text: No. The goal is confidence through small, checkable steps.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's return to something you mentioned earlier, because it appears here too: what should learners do when tools or pages change?

speaker: ALEX
text: First, do not assume that a difference means you failed. Instead, use an exploration pattern. Check the URL and browser tab title. Move by headings, landmarks, tabs, and button names instead of relying on visual order. Open keyboard shortcut help -- on GitHub that's question mark -- or use the VS Code Command Palette if shortcuts differ. Read nearby labels before activating a control. And if needed, ask a facilitator or file a curriculum issue including the page link, the step that differed, and what you heard or observed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the durable skill is orient, verify, continue.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now we get to the actual starting sequence. Step one: know your starting place.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Before Day 1, complete the pre-workshop setup chapter. That chapter helps you create or verify your GitHub account, configure accessibility settings, choose a browser, install Git and VS Code, and confirm your screen reader setup.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if setup is incomplete?

speaker: ALEX
text: Tell a facilitator early. Recovery time and support are built into the workshop.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step two is accepting the GitHub Classroom assignment. Can you walk us through that in plain language?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. At the start of Day 1, the facilitator gives you a GitHub Classroom assignment link. It usually begins with classroom dot github dot com slash a slash.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And you should open it in the browser where you're already signed in to GitHub.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. Then, if GitHub asks you to authorize GitHub Classroom, activate the authorize button. If you're asked to choose your name from a roster, select your name. If your name is missing, use the skip option and tell the facilitator. Then activate Accept this assignment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then wait.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. GitHub Classroom needs a moment to create your private repository. Refresh until the repository link appears, open it, and bookmark it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the repository name usually follows a pattern.

speaker: ALEX
text: Something like learning-room-your-username. That repository is your personal practice space.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which takes us to step three: understanding the Learning Room itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: The Learning Room is a private repository created from a template. Everyone begins with the same materials, but your work belongs to you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Why is that such a useful design?

speaker: ALEX
text: Because it gives you a safe place to practice real GitHub workflows. You have your own issues, branches, commits, and pull requests. Other students do not see your work unless pairing is intentional. Mistakes are expected and recoverable. Bot feedback is educational, not punitive. And challenge issues unlock in sequence, so you always know the next step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I love the phrase guided practice studio.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's exactly what it is. Real GitHub, but with scaffolding.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step four is finding Challenge 1.

speaker: ALEX
text: Once the Learning Room is created, the Student Progression Bot opens your first challenge issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So what does the learner do?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open your Learning Room repository. Go to the Issues tab -- on GitHub the shortcut is G then I. Find the issue titled Challenge 1: Find Your Way Around. Open it and read the issue body from top to bottom. Follow the checklist. Post your evidence in the requested place, either in the evidence field or as a comment. Then close the challenge issue when the instructions tell you to close it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And closing one challenge triggers the next.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You do not have to hunt through the whole course wondering what comes next. The sequence is designed to reveal the next step when you finish the current one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step five is tool choice, and I think this is where learners sometimes get anxious. They worry there's one right tool.

speaker: ALEX
text: There isn't. The workshop teaches the workflow first, then gives you multiple tool paths.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we do the plain-language version?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. GitHub.com in the browser is best for Day 1, issues, pull requests, repository navigation, and reviews. github.dev is best when you want a VS Code-style editor in the browser without installing anything; on many repository pages, press period to open it. VS Code desktop is best for Day 2, local Git, Copilot, extensions, and deeper editing. GitHub Desktop is best if you want a desktop Git workflow without typing Git commands. GitHub CLI is best if you prefer terminal workflows or want automation later.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And whichever tool you use, the important thing is understanding the contribution workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes: issue, branch, change, commit, pull request, review, merge. Tools may differ, but that conceptual path stays consistent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step six is especially for screen reader users: what to listen for.

speaker: ALEX
text: When you feel lost, listen for structure before taking action. Useful signals include the page title, the repository name heading, landmark names like main content or repository navigation, tab names like Code, Issues, and Pull requests, the issue title, the pull request title, the branch name, button names, field labels, and bot comments or check results.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm unsure where I am, I shouldn't panic. I should pause and navigate by headings or landmarks.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Re-orienting is part of the workflow. It is not evidence that you're doing something wrong.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Step seven is using the support built into the course.

speaker: ALEX
text: And there's quite a lot of it. Every challenge issue includes instructions and evidence prompts. Every chapter has an If You Get Stuck section. Every challenge has a reference solution in the solutions folder. Gandalf bot posts feedback on pull requests. The Student Progression Bot opens the next challenge when you finish the current one. Facilitators and peers are part of the support system too.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the advice is: don't silently restart from scratch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Read the latest bot message, check the challenge issue, and ask for help with the link to the page where you got stuck.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then step eight is a first success check.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You are ready to continue when you can say four things: I can open my Learning Room repository. I can find the Issues tab. I can open Challenge 1. And I know where to post my evidence and how to ask for help.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's such a healthy checkpoint, because it doesn't require mastery of everything.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You do not need to understand every GitHub feature before beginning. You just need enough orientation to take the next step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So where should learners go next if they want the gentlest path?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use this order: pre-workshop setup, choose your tools, understand GitHub, navigate repositories, understand the Learning Room, and then complete your Challenge 1 issue in your own repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And I think the final line of that guide is worth repeating in spirit.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You belong here. The path is meant to stay explicit, and the course keeps giving you the next step.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, let's make sure we include the administrative details learners may need later.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good idea. For current factual reference, use GitHub Docs and the GitHub Changelog. The course materials also include section-level source maps showing which official sources back which sections.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if a learner needs immediate practical backup during the workshop?

speaker: ALEX
text: Keep the reference materials nearby: glossary, screen reader cheat sheet, troubleshooting, quick reference, FAQ, and the resources collection. If you're still stuck, open a support issue with clear details about what you tried, what happened, what you expected, and which screen reader and operating system you're using.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the through line of this whole episode is: this workshop is real, but it is also structured.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Real tools, real workflows, real contributions, but also clear guidance, private practice space, progressive challenges, bot feedback, accessible orientation strategies, and multiple ways to continue when something changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels like a good place to land. You do not have to know everything. You do need a starting place.

speaker: ALEX
text: And now you have one. Open your setup chapter if you haven't completed it yet. Accept your GitHub Classroom assignment when your facilitator provides it. Bookmark your Learning Room. Find Challenge 1. And keep moving one checkable step at a time.

speaker: JAMIE
text: We'll meet you in the next part.
